<title level="1" right="Rules">[Ammunition](/Rules.aspx?ID=3193)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 255

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
These magic items are ammunition for ranged weapons. Each item's stat block includes an Ammunition entry that lists which type of ammunition it can be [Crafted](/Actions.aspx?ID=2385) as, or “any” if it's not limited to any particular type. All stat blocks for ammunition omit the Usage and Bulk entries; use the standard rules for the type of ammunition to determine reloading times and Bulk.

When using magic ammunition, use your ranged weapon's fundamental runes to determine the attack modifier and damage dice. Don't add the effects of your weapon's property runes unless the ammunition states otherwise—the ammunition creates its own effects. Magic ammunition deals damage on a hit normally in addition to any listed effects unless its description states otherwise.

Regardless of whether an attack with magic ammunition hits or misses, launching the ammunition consumes its magic. Magic ammunition is made of normal materials, not precious materials, unless stated otherwise.

[Click here for the Magical Ammunition category page](/Equipment.aspx?Category=15&Subcategory=16).
</column>

</column>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Activated Ammunition](/Rules.aspx?ID=3194)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 255

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
If magic ammunition doesn't have an Activate entry, it's activated automatically when it's launched. Types of magic ammunition that have an Activate entry must be activated with additional actions before being used. Once you activate the ammunition, you must shoot it before the end of your turn. Otherwise, it deactivates (but it isn't consumed) and you must activate it again before you can use it. If you shoot the ammunition without activating it first, it functions as non-magical ammunition and is still consumed.

The action required to activate the ammunition doesn't alter how many actions it takes to [reload](/Rules.aspx?ID=2196). For example, you could activate a [beacon shot](/Equipment.aspx?ID=2921) arrow by touching it with 1 action, then draw and shoot the arrow as part of a [Strike](/Actions.aspx?ID=2306) as normal. For a beacon shot bolt, you could activate it, load it into a [crossbow](/Weapons.aspx?ID=425), then shoot it, or load it into the crossbow, then activate it, and then shoot it.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Oils](/Rules.aspx?ID=3195)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 257

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Oils are magical gels, ointments, pastes, or salves that are typically applied to an object (though sometimes to a creature). They're typically found in vials (similar to potions) or small tins. They have the [oil](/Traits.aspx?ID=663) trait. Like other consumables, oils are used up when activated.

Applying an oil usually takes two hands: one to hold the jar containing the oil and another to extract the oil and apply it. You can only apply an oil to an item or creature within your reach. Because the process is so thorough, it is usually impossible to apply an oil to an unwilling target or an item in the possession of an unwilling target unless that target is [paralyzed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=85), [petrified](/Conditions.aspx?ID=87), or [unconscious](/Conditions.aspx?ID=95).

[Click here for the Oils category page](/Equipment.aspx?Category=15&Subcategory=17).

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 259

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A potion is a magical liquid that is activated when you drink it, which uses it up. Potions have the [potion](/Traits.aspx?ID=672) trait. You can activate a potion with an [Interact](/Actions.aspx?ID=2297) action as you drink it or feed it to another creature. You can feed a potion only to a creature that is within reach and willing or otherwise so helpless that it can’t resist. You usually need only one hand to consume a potion or feed it to another creature.

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Scrolls](/Rules.aspx?ID=3197)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 262

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A scroll contains a single spell that you can cast without having to expend a spell slot. A scroll can be [Crafted](/Actions.aspx?ID=2385) to contain nearly any spell. The exceptions are [cantrips](/Rules.aspx?ID=2227), [focus spells](/Rules.aspx?ID=2228), and [rituals](/Rules.aspx?ID=2255), none of which can be put on scrolls. The spell on a scroll can be cast only once, and the scroll is destroyed as part of the casting. The spell on the scroll is cast at a particular spell rank, as determined by the scroll. For instance, a scroll of [_force barrage_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1536) (1st rank) can be used to cast the 1st-rank version of _force barrage_, but not a force barrage heightened to 2nd rank. If no rank is listed, the scroll can be used to cast the spell at its lowest rank.

If you find a scroll, you can try to figure out what spell it contains. If the spell is a common spell from your spell list or a spell you know, you can spend a single [Recall Knowledge](/Actions.aspx?ID=2367) action and automatically succeed at identifying the scroll's spell. If it's not, you must [Identify Magic](/Actions.aspx?ID=2365).

[Click here for the Scrolls category page](/Equipment.aspx?Category=15&Subcategory=52).
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Casting a Spell from a Scroll](/Rules.aspx?ID=3198)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 262

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Casting a Spell from a scroll requires holding the scroll in one hand and activating it with a [Cast a Spell](/Actions.aspx?ID=2734) activity using the normal number of actions for that spell. The spell must appear on your spell list. Because you're the one Casting the Spell, use your spell attack modifier and spell DC. The spell also gains the appropriate trait for your tradition ([arcane](/Traits.aspx?ID=534), [divine](/Traits.aspx?ID=579), [occult](/Traits.aspx?ID=662), or [primal](/Traits.aspx?ID=676)).

Any physical costs are provided when a scroll is created, so you don't need to provide them when casting from a scroll. If the spell requires a [locus](/Rules.aspx?ID=2234), you must have that locus to Cast the Spell from a scroll.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Varying Statistics](/Rules.aspx?ID=3200)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 262

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
The [magic scroll](/Equipment.aspx?ID=2962) stat block indicates the item level and Price of a scroll, both of which are based on the rank of the spell in the scroll. Any costs to [Cast the Spell](/Actions.aspx?ID=2734) are added to the scroll's Price when the scroll is crafted, so a scroll containing a spell with a Cost entry will have a higher Price than what's on the table. The scroll's rarity matches the spell's rarity.

The traits for a scroll vary based on the spell it contains. A scroll always has the [consumable](/Traits.aspx?ID=564), [magical](/Traits.aspx?ID=644), and [scroll](/Traits.aspx?ID=688) traits, plus any traits that might be relevant to identifying the item, such as [illusion](/Traits.aspx?ID=629) or [fire](/Traits.aspx?ID=604). The GM determines the extra traits, if necessary.
</column>

</column>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Crafting a Scroll](/Rules.aspx?ID=3201)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 262

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
The process to [Craft](/Actions.aspx?ID=2385) a scroll is much like that to Craft any other magic item. When you begin the crafting process, choose a spell to put into the scroll. You must either [Cast that Spell](/Actions.aspx?ID=2734) during the crafting process, or someone else must do so in your presence. Casting that Spell doesn't produce its normal effects; instead, the magic is trapped inside the scroll. The casting must come from a spellcaster expending a spell slot. You can't Craft a scroll from a spell produced from another magic item, for example. The caster must provide any cost of the spell. A scroll's rarity is the same as that of the spell it contains.

Like other consumables, scrolls can be crafted in batches of four. All scrolls of one batch must contain the same spell at the same rank, and you must provide one casting for each scroll that is crafted.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Talismans](/Rules.aspx?ID=3203)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 263

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
An item with the [talisman](/Traits.aspx?ID=709) trait is a magical charm, gem, stone, or other small object affixed to armor, a shield, or a weapon (called the affixed item). Each talisman holds a sliver of combat knowledge or magical energy that can be unleashed for a momentary boost of power or enhanced ability. Many talismans can be activated as a free action when you use a particular action or activity. A talisman is a [consumable](/Traits.aspx?ID=564) item and has the talisman trait.

You must be wielding or wearing an item to activate a talisman attached to it. Once activated, a talisman burns out permanently, usually crumbling into a fine dust.
</column>

An item with the [expandable](/Traits.aspx?ID=475) trait increases to a specific size when activated. <title level="1" right="Rules">[Apex Items](/Rules.aspx?ID=3208)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 270

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
When you [Invest an Item](/Actions.aspx?ID=2799) that has the [apex](/Traits.aspx?ID=532) trait, it improves one of your attributes, either increasing the attribute’s modifier by 1 or to a total of +4, whichever would give you a higher value. This gives you all the benefits of the new attribute modifier until the investiture ends. For example, in addition to increasing rolls you make using the attribute, increasing your Intelligence lets you become trained in an additional skill and learn a new language, and increasing your Constitution gives you more Hit Points.

An apex item grants this benefit only the first time it’s invested within a 24-hour period, and you can benefit from only one apex item at a time. If you attempt to invest an apex item when you already have one invested, you don’t gain the attribute modifier increase, though you do gain any other effects of Investing the Item.

[Click here for the Apex Items category page](/Equipment.aspx?Category=42).

You might want to acquire items that benefit a creature that assists you, such as an [animal companion](/Rules.aspx?ID=2113), [familiar](/Rules.aspx?ID=2121), or [bonded animal](/Feats.aspx?ID=5127). These items have the [companion](/Traits.aspx?ID=558) trait, meaning they function only for animal companions, familiars, and similar creatures. Normally, these are the only items a companion can use. Other items can qualify at the GM's discretion, but a companion can never [Activate an Item](/Rules.aspx?ID=3139).

Any worn companion item needs to be invested. However, your companion needs to invest it, rather than you doing so. This requires you to use the [Invest an Item](/Actions.aspx?ID=289) activity alongside your companion, helping them attune to the item and ensuring it is properly fit. A companion has an investiture limit of two items (instead of the 10-item limit a player character has).

[Click here for the Companion Items category page](/Equipment.aspx?Category=41&Subcategory=43).
</column>

<aside>
<title level="2" noclass="true" icon="">The Structure Trait</title>

An item with the [structure](/Traits.aspx?ID=704) trait creates a magical building or other structure when activated. The item must be activated on a plot of land free of other structures. The structure adapts to the natural terrain, adopting the structural requirements for being built there. The structure adjusts around small features such as ponds or spires of rock, but it can't be created on water or other nonsolid surfaces. If activated on snow, sand dunes, or other soft surfaces with a solid surface underneath, the structure's foundation (if any) reaches the solid ground. If an item with this trait is activated on a solid but unstable surface, such as a swamp or an area plagued by tremors, roll a DC 3 flat check each day; on a failure, the structure begins to sink or collapse.

The structure doesn't harm creatures within the area when it appears, and it can't be created within a crowd or in a densely populated area. Any creature inadvertently caught inside the structure when the item is activated ends up unharmed inside the complete structure and always has a clear path of escape. A creature inside the structure when the activation ends isn't harmed, and it lands harmlessly on the ground if it was on an upper level of the structure.
</aside>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Staves](/Rules.aspx?ID=3211)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 278

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A magical staff is an indispensable accessory for a spellcaster. A staff is tied to a person during a preparation process, after which the preparer, and only the preparer, can use the staff to produce magic. The spells that can be cast from a staff are listed in bullet points organized by rank. Staves can be found in multiple types, with more powerful types containing more spells—such a staff always contains the spells of all lower-level types of the staff, in addition to the spells listed in its own entry. All magical staves have the [staff](/Traits.aspx?ID=700) trait.

[Click here for the Staves category page](/Equipment.aspx?Category=32).
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Casting Spells from a Staff](/Rules.aspx?ID=3212)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 278

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A staff gains charges when someone [prepares](/Rules.aspx?ID=3214) it for the day. The person who prepared a staff can expend the charges to cast spells from it. You can [Cast a Spell](/Actions.aspx?ID=2734) from a staff only if you have that spell on your spell list, are able to cast spells of the appropriate rank or higher, and expend a number of charges from the staff equal to the spell's rank. Casting a Spell from a staff requires holding the staff (typically in one hand) and Activating the staff by Casting the Spell, which takes the spell's normal number of actions.

Use your spell attack modifier and spell DC when Casting a Spell from a staff. The spell gains the appropriate trait for your magical tradition and can be affected by any modifications you can normally make when casting spells, such as [spellshape](/Traits.aspx?ID=513) feats. You must provide any [cost or locus](/Rules.aspx?ID=2234) required by the spell, or you fail to cast it.

Prepared spellcasters and spontaneous spellcasters each have a unique way of altering how their staves gain charges and the ways they can be used (see the [Prepared Spellcasters](/Rules.aspx?ID=3215) and [Spontaneous Spellcasters](/Rules.aspx?ID=3216) sections below).
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Preparing a Staff](/Rules.aspx?ID=3214)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 278

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
During your [daily preparations](/Rules.aspx?ID=2575), you can prepare a staff to add charges to it for free. When you do so, that staff gains a number of charges equal to the rank of your highest spell slot. You don’t need to expend any spells to add charges in this way. No one can prepare more than one staff per day, nor can a staff be prepared by more than one person per day. If the charges aren’t used within 24 hours, they’re lost, and preparing the staff anew removes any charges previously stored in it. You can prepare a staff only if you have at least one of the staff’s spells on your spell list.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Prepared Spellcasters](/Rules.aspx?ID=3215)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 278

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A prepared spellcaster—such as a [cleric](/Classes.aspx?ID=33), [druid](/Classes.aspx?ID=34), [witch](/Classes.aspx?ID=38), or [wizard](/Classes.aspx?ID=39)—can place some of their own magic in a staff to increase its number of charges. When a prepared spellcaster prepares a staff, they can expend a spell slot to add a number of charges equal to the rank of the spell. They can’t expend more than one spell in this way each day. For example, if a wizard can cast 3rd-rank spells and prepared a staff, the staff would gain 3 charges, but wizard could increase this to 6 by expending one of their 3rd-rank spells, 5 by expending a 2nd-rank spell, or 4 by expending a 1st-rank spell.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Spontaneous Spellcasters](/Rules.aspx?ID=3216)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 278

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A spontaneous spellcaster—such as a [bard](/Classes.aspx?ID=32), [oracle](/Classes.aspx?ID=14), or [sorcerer](/Classes.aspx?ID=11)—can reduce the number of charges it takes to Activate a staff by supplementing it with their own energy. When a spontaneous spellcaster Activates a staff, they can expend 1 charge from the staff and one of their spell slots to cast a spell from the staff of the same rank (or lower) as the expended spell slot. This doesn’t change the number of actions it takes to cast the spell. For example, if a sorcerer can cast 3rd-rank spells and prepared a staff, the staff would gain 3 charges. They could expend 1 charge and one of their 3rd-rank spell slots to cast a 3rd-rank spell from the staff, or 1 charge and one of their 2nd-rank spell slots to cast a 2nd-rank spell from the staff. They could still expend 3 charges from the staff to cast a 3rd-rank spell from it without using any of their own slots, just like any other spellcaster.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Wands](/Rules.aspx?ID=3218)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 282

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Short, slender items typically made of wood, wands let you cast a specific spell without expending a spell slot. They can be used once per day, but can be [overcharged](/Rules.aspx?ID=3220) to attempt to cast them again at great risk. Each wand holds a spell of a certain rank, determined when the wand is created. [Cantrips](/Rules.aspx?ID=2227), [focus spells](/Rules.aspx?ID=2228), and [rituals](/Rules.aspx?ID=2255) can't be placed in wands.

If you find a wand, you can try to deduce what spell is in it. If the spell is a common spell from your spell list or is a spell you know, you can use a single [Recall Knowledge](/Actions.aspx?ID=2367) action and automatically succeed. If it's not, you must [Identify Magic](/Actions.aspx?ID=2365).
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Casting Spells from a Wand](/Rules.aspx?ID=3219)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 282

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A wand contains a spell that can be cast once per day. Casting a spell from a wand requires holding the wand in one hand and activating the item with a [Cast a Spell](/Actions.aspx?ID=2734) activity using the normal number of actions for the spell.

To cast a spell from a wand, it must be on your spell list. Because you're the one casting the spell, use your spell attack modifier and spell DC. The spell is of your spellcasting tradition.

If a spell requires a [locus](/Rules.aspx?ID=2234), you must still have that locus to cast the spell from a wand, and if the spell has a cost, you must still pay that cost to cast the spell from a wand.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Overcharging a Wand](/Rules.aspx?ID=3220)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 282

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
After the spell is cast from the wand for the day, you can attempt to cast it one more time—overcharging the wand at the risk of destroying it. Cast the Spell again, then roll a DC 10 flat check. On a success, the wand is broken. On a failure, the wand is destroyed. If anyone tries to overcharge a wand when it’s already been overcharged that day, the wand is automatically destroyed (even if it had been repaired) and no spell is cast.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Varying Statistics](/Rules.aspx?ID=3222)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 282

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Each type of wand has a level and Price determined by the spell's rank. The wand's rarity matches the spell's rarity, and it has its spell's traits. A wand has the normal Hardness, BT, and HP of a thin item of its material (see [Materials](/Rules.aspx?ID=3188)).

The traits for a wand vary based on the spell it contains. A wand always has the [magical](/Traits.aspx?ID=644) and [wand](/Traits.aspx?ID=731) traits, plus any traits that might be relevant to identifying the item, such as [illusion](/Traits.aspx?ID=629) or [fire](/Traits.aspx?ID=604). The GM determines the extra traits.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Crafting a Wand](/Rules.aspx?ID=3223)</title>

**Source** [GM Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=218) pg. 282

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
For the most part, the process to [Craft](/Actions.aspx?ID=2385) a wand is like that to Craft any other magic item. When you begin the crafting process, choose a spell to put into the wand. You have to either cast that spell during the process, or someone else must do so in your presence. That spell doesn't have its normal effects; instead, the magic is captured inside the wand. The caster doesn't need to pay any cost of the spell.

The casting must come from a spellcaster expending a spell slot. You can't make a wand from a spell that comes from another magic item, for example. A wand's rarity is the same as that of the spell it contains.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>
